feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The recent advancements in cryptographic proof aggregation have introduced a significant development that allows an untrusted third party to consolidate multiple proofs into a single constant-sized proof, approximately 200 KB in size. This technique utilizes risc0's succinct receipt type, which employs a recursion circuit to maintain the proof's size regardless of the number of proofs aggregated. The core functionality hinges on a special batch verified guest program that processes a series of fixed-size claims, verifies their validity using the recursion gadget, and constructs a Merkle tree to commit to each claim, ultimately generating a new batch proof.
This innovative approach supports heterogeneous claim aggregation, allowing for the combination of distinct leaves and another batch root within the same structure. This flexibility enables the aggregation of different types of statements, such as those from bip-32 taproot or xpriv/xpub claims, without requiring uniformity among the statements. However, this method does have limitations, notably that the size of the claim tree increases with each additional batch and leaf aggregated, resulting in a larger proof size necessary for final inclusion verification.
Alternatives to the current method are being considered, including switching to either a Sparse Merkle Tree (SMT) or a Merkle Mountain Range (MMR), which may offer different advantages in terms of efficiency and scalability. Performance metrics from initial tests have been documented, showing varying prove times and memory requirements based on the number of leaves aggregated. For instance, aggregating two leaves requires 5 seconds and 3 GB of RAM, while sixteen leaves require 33 seconds and 11 GB of RAM.
For more detailed insights into these developments and the underlying technologies, further documentation and implementation details can be accessed through several resources: [recursive proving](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/main/docs/recursive-proving.md), [guest batch](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/d6e9ee2f806f69c4307eeed9394aaa8ac4beaabe/guest_batch/main.goL61-L70), [Merkle Mountain Range sketch](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/main/docs/mmr-accumulator-sketch.md), [Sparse Merkle Tree plan](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/main/docs/sparse-accumulator-plan.md), and [batch aggregation scaling results](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/main/docs/batch-aggregation.mdscaling-results). These resources provide comprehensive information on the technical aspects and potential future directions of proof aggregation technology.
